1: YHWH ʾĕlōhê yĕshûʿātî — O LORD, God of my salvation. Even in the darkest psalm, the address names God as ʾĕlōhê yĕshûʿātî (God of my salvation) — the title contains the entire hope that the psalm cannot find. yôm-ṣāʿaqtî ballaylāh negdekhā — I cry out to you day and night. Yôm and laylāh together (day and night) — unbroken, continuous prayer. The contrast with v18's conclusion (darkness as companion) is complete: prayer fills both day and night, yet receives no answer presented in the psalm.
2: tābôʾ lĕphānêkhā tĕphillātî — let my prayer come before you. Bôʾ lĕphānêkhā (to come before you — to gain audience, to reach the divine presence) — the prayer as a traveler seeking admission to God's court. haṭṭēh ʾoznĕkhā lĕrinnātî — turn your ear toward my cry. Rinnāh (cry, shout — can be of joy or of distress; here clearly the latter) — the ringing cry of acute distress seeking divine attention.
3: kî-śābiʿāh bĕrāʿôt nafshî — for my soul is saturated with troubles. Śābaʿ (to be satisfied, sated — used normally of having one's fill of food or good things) here applied ironically to rāʿôt (evils, troubles) — the soul has been filled to capacity with evil, as a container that can take no more. wĕḥayyay lishʾôl higgîʿû — and my life draws near to Sheol. The verb nāgaʿ (to touch, reach) — life has made contact with Sheol's boundary.
4: nĕḥshabtî ʿim-yôrĕdê bôr — I am reckoned among those who go down to the pit. Ḥāshab (niphal: to be counted, reckoned among) — the social/legal accounting: the Psalmist has been placed in the category of the dying/dead while still alive. Bôr (pit — the underworld, Sheol's concrete image). hāyîtî kĕgeber ʾên-ʾāyāl — I have become like a man with no strength at all. ʾĀyāl (strength, vigor — from ʾayil: ram, the symbol of physical power) — utterly drained.
5: bammētîm ḥophshî — like one cast loose among the dead. Ḥophshî (free, released — the word for a freed slave) — the paradox: among the dead one is free from life's obligations and relationships, but this freedom is the freedom of total abandonment. kĕmô ḥălālîm shōkĕbê qeber ʾăsher lōʾ zĕkhartām ʿôd — like the slain who lie in the grave — those you no longer remember, cut off from your hand. The terrifying clause: those in the grave are those lōʾ zĕkhartām ʿôd (whom you no longer remember). In Hebrew thought, divine memory sustains existence; to be forgotten by God is to cease to exist in any meaningful sense.
6: shattanî bĕbôr taḥtiyyôt — you have placed me in the lowest pit. The hiphil of shît (to place, set) — God is explicitly the agent of this placement, not merely an observer. bĕmaḥăshakkîm bimĕṣôlôt — in utter darkness, in the depths below. Maḥăshak (darkness, the dark place — the characteristic condition of Sheol) + mĕṣôlāh (deep place, abyss — used of the depths of the sea in Exodus 15:5; Micah 7:19). The imagery of pit + darkness + depths combines all Sheol metaphors.
7: ʿālay sāmĕkhāh ḥămātekhā — your wrath presses down hard on me. Sāmak (qal: to lean upon, press down — the same verb used of laying hands on a sacrifice; here God's wrath is the weight pressing down on the Psalmist). wĕkhol-mishbārêkhā ʿinnîtā selāh — and you have battered me with all your waves. Mishbār (breaker, crashing wave — from shābar: to break) — the same word as the breakers of the sea (Psalms 42:7; Psalms 93:3). Divine wrath experienced as a battering sea. The selah marks a moment of overwhelming weight.
8: hirḥaqtā mĕyuddaʿay mimmennî — you have pushed my companions far away from me. The hiphil of rāḥaq (to remove to a distance) — God as the agent of social isolation. shattanî tôʿēbôt lāmô — you have made me repulsive to them. Tôʿēbāh (abomination, something repulsive) — the Psalmist has become the thing his companions find morally/ritually unacceptable. kāluʾ wĕlōʾ ʾēṣēʾ — I am sealed in and cannot get out. Kālaʾ (niphal: to be shut up, imprisoned) — trapped in both the isolation of illness and the approaching tomb.
9: ʿênî dāʾăbāh minnî ʿōnî — my eye wastes away with grief. Dāʾab (to pine, waste away — the slow, sustained wasting of grief, not the sudden shock of acute loss). qĕrāʾtîkhā YHWH kol-hayyôm shēṭaḥtî ʾēlêkhā khappāy — O LORD, I call to you every day; I spread out my hands toward you. The spread hands (kappayim: palms) in supplication — the physical gesture of prayer, maintained despite receiving no visible answer. The persistence of prayer against the silence is itself an act of faith.
10: hălammētîm taʿăśeh pheleʾ — do you perform wonders for the dead? The series of questions in vv10-12 are not truly interrogative — they expect the answer 'no' in Sheol-theology, but the question is whether YHWH might break the rule for the Psalmist. Pheleʾ (wonder — the Exodus miracle word) applied to death: can the God who works wonders in history also work them in death? ʾim-rĕphāʾîm yāqûmû yôdûkhā selāh — can the departed arise and praise you? Rĕphāʾîm (the departed spirits, the shades of the dead — a term for Sheol's inhabitants). The selah marks the unanswerable weight of the question.
11: hăyĕsuppār baqqeber ḥasdekā — is your steadfast love announced in the grave? The ḥesed that the Psalmist knows from experience — is it communicated in the grave? ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bāʾăbadon — is your faithfulness spoken of in Abaddon? ʾĂbadon (destruction, the realm of the dead — from ʾābad: to perish; appears in Job 26:6; Psalms 28:22; Proverbs 15:11; Psalms 27:20; Revelation 9:11 as Apollyon). The question pairs ḥesed and ʾĕmûnāh (steadfast love and faithfulness) — the two great covenant attributes — against Sheol and Abaddon.
12: hăyiwwādaʿ baḥōshek pilʾekā — will your wonders be known in the darkness? Ḥōshek (darkness — the characteristic condition of Sheol, as in v6). wĕṣidqātĕkhā bĕʾereṣ nĕshiyyāh — will your righteousness be heard of in the land of forgetting? ʾEreṣ nĕshiyyāh (the land of forgetting) — a unique name for Sheol found only here in the OT. Nĕshiyyāh (from shākah: to forget) — Sheol as the place where memory is lost, where the dead forget and are forgotten. The cosmic geography of oblivion.
13: waʾănî ʾēlêkhā YHWH shiwwaʿtî — but I, O LORD, cry out to you. The contrast particle waʾănî (but I) — against all the rhetorical questions (which expect the answer: no, God does not act in Sheol), the Psalmist continues to pray. ûbaboqer tĕphillātî tĕqaddĕmekā — in the morning my prayer comes before you. Qāddam (to come before, to meet — to intercept) — the morning prayer rushing ahead to meet God before the day's silence descends again. The morning prayer as the daily resumption of hope.
14: lāmāh YHWH tiznaḥ nafshî — O LORD, why do you reject my soul? Zānaḥ (to reject, cast off — the communal lament verb of Psalms 74:1; Psalms 79:5) applied to the individual soul. tastîr pānêkhā mimmennî — why do you hide your face from me? The two great divine absences compressed into one verse: rejection (zānaḥ) and hidden face (hester pānim). These are the standard vocabulary of divine abandonment — the Psalmist names what is happening with theological precision.
15: ʿānî waggōwēaʿ minnōʿar — I have been afflicted and near death since my youth. The duration of the suffering is not a recent crisis but a lifelong condition. nāśāʾtî ʾêmêkhā ʾāphûnāh — I suffer your terrors; I am overcome. ʾêmāh (terror, dread — the overwhelming fear produced by the divine presence in its terrifying aspect). ʾāphûnāh (I am overcome, I am helpless — possibly from phûg: to grow numb, be powerless) — the terror has left him paralyzed.
16: ʿālay ʿābĕrû ḥărônêkhā — your fierce wrath has swept over me. ʿĀbar (to pass over — as a flood sweeps over its banks; the divine anger as an inundating force). biʿûtêkhā ṣimmĕtûnî — your terrors have destroyed me. Biʿûtîm (terrors — from bāʿat: to terrify, overwhelm; the same root as the Psalmist's paralysis in v15). Ṣāmat (to put an end to, silence, destroy — used of the destruction of the wicked in Psalms 18:40; Psalms 94:23) — now applied to the righteous sufferer.
17: ʾibbĕnî khammayim kol-hayyôm — they surround me like water all day long. The terrors personified as surrounding waters — the drowning metaphor that the sea-imagery of v7 anticipates. hiqqîpû ʿālay yaḥad — they close in on me together. Nāqaph (to close in, encircle — used of encircling enemies, Job 1:5; Isaiah 29:1) — a coordinated encirclement with no exit point.
18: hirḥaqtā mimmennî ʾōhēb wārēaʿ — you have put far from me those who love me and my friends. The complete desolation of relationship: ʾōhēb (lover, one who loves) and rēaʿ (friend, companion) both removed by divine action. mĕyuddaʿay maḥshākh — my companions are in darkness. The final words of the psalm. Unlike every other lament psalm, Psalm 88 ends without resolution, without a vow of praise, without a turn to confidence. The only companion remaining is darkness (maḥshākh). This is the Psalter's record of the experience of unresolved suffering — a permission to name what is, without forcing a liturgical resolution that has not come.
